list: The data members will be valid for as long as the struct (or class) is.The value of those data members won’t change unexpectedly.
The value of those data members won’t change unexpectedly.

content.text: Best practiceIn most cases, we want our structs (and classes) to be owners. The easiest way to enable this is to ensure each data member has an owning type (e.g. not a viewer, pointer, or reference).
In most cases, we want our structs (and classes) to be owners. The easiest way to enable this is to ensure each data member has an owning type (e.g. not a viewer, pointer, or reference).
Author’s notePractice safe structs. Don’t let your member dangle.
Practice safe structs. Don’t let your member dangle.

The size of short is 2 bytes
The size of int is 4 bytes
The size of double is 8 bytes
The size of Foo is 16 bytes

content.text: For advanced readersThe reason compilers may add padding is beyond the scope of this tutorial, but readers who want to learn more can read aboutdata structure alignmenton Wikipedia. This is optional reading and not required to understand structures or C++!

The reason compilers may add padding is beyond the scope of this tutorial, but readers who want to learn more can read aboutdata structure alignmenton Wikipedia. This is optional reading and not required to understand structures or C++!

This program prints:12
8Note thatFoo1andFoo2have the same members, the only difference being the declaration order. YetFoo1is 50% larger due to the added padding.TipYou can minimize padding by defining your members in decreasing order of size.The C++ compiler is not allowed to reorder members, so this has to be done manually.Next lesson13.12Member selection with pointers and referencesBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson13.10Passing and returning structs
